findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A2 Live Fetch
findings.title: Ranorex citation resolves but does not support the form-selection claim attributed to it

evidence.excerpt: Rule-oriented vs scenario-oriented. Some teams default to checklists, others to Given/When/Then [[7]](#ref-7).
Default to the checklist; reach for scenarios when a flow is genuinely easier to read as Given/When/Then than as rules [[7]](#ref-7).
[7] Ranorex. "When to Use Given-When-Then Acceptance Criteria." ranorex.com (accessed 2026-06-30). [vendor]

findings.why_it_matters: The task instructions require that a citation that resolves but does not support its claim be treated as High severity, worse than a dead link. The companion attributes its core form-selection guidance ('some teams default to checklists, others to GWT'; 'default to the checklist; reach for scenarios when...') to the Ranorex article four times across lines 24, 62, 96, 122, and 124. The fetch confirmed the Ranorex article 'does not address scenarios versus rule checklists' and 'concentrates on implementing the given-when-then structure without discussing alternatives' - it is a vendor piece promoting Ranorex tooling, not a comparative treatment. Readers and agents who follow the citation to verify the recommendation find nothing supporting it, directly undermining the 'researched, not remembered' core claim at its most instructive point.
findings.recommendation: Replace the Ranorex citation for the form-selection guidance with a practitioner or BDD textbook source that explicitly compares checklist and GWT formats (for example, Gojko Adzic's BDD writing or the Cucumber BDD documentation). Ranorex may be retained for any Gherkin-keyword claims where the article does provide content. If no adequate source exists, label the recommendation author judgment per methodology principle 2.

findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A2 Live Fetch / A3 Research-Log Tracing
findings.title: Three SVPG companion references assert '(accessed 2026-06-30)' for sources that returned HTTP 403

evidence.excerpt: [2] Marty Cagan. "Revisiting the Product Spec" ... Silicon Valley Product Group, 2007 (accessed 2026-06-30). [practitioner]
[3] Marty Cagan. "How To Write a Good PRD" ... 2024 (accessed 2026-06-30). [practitioner]
[4] Marty Cagan. "Discovery vs. Documentation" ... (accessed 2026-06-30). [practitioner]
Cagan, 'Revisiting the Product Spec' | Search excerpt (page 403-blocked to fetch)
SVPG pages ... returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch; their substance was captured from search excerpts and corroborated against the master catalog

findings.why_it_matters: The convention '(accessed DATE)' in scholarly and practitioner citation practice means the URL was successfully retrieved on that date. Applying it to pages that returned HTTP 403 creates a false credibility signal in the companion's reference list: the reader and any downstream agent sees three references with access dates implying the sources were read directly. The research log is admirably honest about this gap, but the companion - which is the deliverable readers see - contradicts that honesty. For a library whose core claim is 'researched, not remembered,' the gap between what the companion implies and what actually happened is a material integrity failure at the citation level. This affects the three most-cited references in the PRD companion, which anchor the library's central product-spec debate.
findings.recommendation: Add a retrieval qualifier to each 403-blocked entry in the companion reference list, such as '(accessed 2026-06-30 via search excerpt; direct fetch blocked HTTP 403)'. This is a one-line fix per entry. Establish a convention in methodology section 6 for citing paywalled or blocked sources so future bundles handle this consistently.

findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A4 Staleness
findings.title: Release notes companion states 'the current version is 1.1.0' for Keep a Changelog when version 1.1.2 had been current for nine months at authoring time

evidence.excerpt: The current version is 1.1.0 [[1]](#ref-1).
[1] Olivier Lacan. "Keep a Changelog," version 1.1.0. keepachangelog.com, 2014 to present (accessed 2026-06-30). [primary]
Recency note: Keep a Changelog 2.0.0 is planned for 2026; this bundle cites 1.1.0 as current and should be re-checked when 2.0.0 ships.

findings.why_it_matters: Methodology principle 4 states 'Recency is a feature.' The changelog on keepachangelog.com shows v1.1.1 was released 2023-03-05 and v1.1.2 was released 2024-09-27 - both more than a year before the June 2026 authoring date. The research log's recency note looks ahead to v2.0.0 but does not acknowledge that two intervening releases had already shipped. The companion's factual assertion 'the current version is 1.1.0' is wrong as stated. Since the release notes bundle cites this spec as its primary normative reference, the error is at the most authoritative point in the document. The reference URL is also hardcoded to the version-specific path /en/1.1.0/ rather than pointing to the latest version.
findings.recommendation: Update the companion text to 'The current version as of this research is 1.1.2 (released September 2024) [[1]](#ref-1).' Update the reference URL from /en/1.1.0/ to /en/1.1.2/ or the redirecting root, and update the access date. Add a staleness-check step to the methodology DoD (section 7) that explicitly asks: 'is the cited version of any versioned standard still the current release?'

findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A1 Citation Census
findings.title: PRD companion carries two padded entries never cited inline; ref 12 carries an inflated tier tag

evidence.excerpt: [8] "Lenny's Product Requirements template." Atlassian Confluence Templates (accessed 2026-06-30). [vendor] - never cited inline
[12] Hustle Badger. "12x PRD Examples and Real PRD Templates." hustlebadger.com (accessed 2026-06-30). [practitioner] - never cited inline
Do not pad the list with sources you did not actually use.
References: numbered, reliability-tagged, no orphan citations, no padded entries

findings.why_it_matters: The DoD (methodology line 200) requires 'no padded entries.' Grep across the entire prd directory finds zero occurrences of [[8]] or [[12]] anywhere in the companion body, confirming both are unused. The gate checks the inline-to-anchor direction only and does not catch this reverse. Additionally, ref-12 (Hustle Badger) is tagged [practitioner], implying a 'recognized independent authority or canonical book/talk' per methodology section 6; Hustle Badger is a general PM content marketing site with no recognized authority status, making the tag an inflation of credibility for a source the companion never even uses.
findings.recommendation: Remove refs 8 and 12 from the PRD companion reference list. If either source was consulted during research and informed a claim not yet explicitly attributed, add an inline citation to the relevant sentence. If retained, retag ref-12 as [vendor] not [practitioner]. Add a CI check that verifies every anchor in a companion's reference list has at least one inline citation pointing to it (the reverse direction the gate currently misses).

findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A2 Live Fetch / A3 Research-Log Tracing
findings.title: PRD companion contains a quoted phrase attributed to Lenny's Newsletter whose body was paywalled and not directly read

evidence.excerpt: nailing it is 'the single most important step in solving any problem' [[7]](#ref-7)
[7] Lenny Rachitsky. "Examples and templates of 1-Pagers and PRDs." Lenny's Newsletter (accessed 2026-06-30). [practitioner]
Search excerpt (body paywalled) ... claims from [7] are kept to the problem-first and one-pager points that the summary states plainly

findings.why_it_matters: Methodology principle 6 (section A4) states: 'Quote sparingly and exactly; attribute every quote.' The phrase 'the single most important step in solving any problem' is formatted as a direct quotation attributed to ref-7. A live fetch of the Lenny's Newsletter URL confirmed 'This post is for paid subscribers' with no substantive content visible to non-subscribers. The research log is transparent that only the public summary was used, but also acknowledges no specific quote from the summary. A quoted phrase attributed to a source whose body was not read cannot be verified as accurate wording from that source and may be a paraphrase or a phrase absorbed from search-result excerpts. The methodology's cite-or-cut and exact-quoting rules apply here.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) obtain subscriber access to verify the exact phrase and update it if needed, or (b) remove the quotation marks to make it a paraphrase and note in the research log that it is paraphrase-from-excerpt, not a direct quote. Option (b) also reduces the claim's force to match what the source actually substantiates. More broadly, add a rule to methodology section 6 prohibiting direct-quote notation for paywalled or search-excerpt-only sources.

findings.dimension: Content quality and research integrity - A1 Citation Census
findings.title: Four reference entries deviate from the methodology's one-source-per-entry format; two combine distinct organizations into a single numbered reference

evidence.excerpt: [5] Connextra team (credited to Rachel Davies and the team). The "As a [role]..." template, 2001. [practitioner] - no URL, no hyperlink
[3] Ron Jeffries. "Card, Conversation, Confirmation," 2001; and Mike Cohn, "User Stories Applied," 2004 ... [practitioner] - two sources, one entry
[6] Appcues, "Release notes examples," and AnnounceKit, "Release Notes Best Practices." appcues.com (accessed 2026-06-30). [vendor] - two organizations, one URL
References are linked, not bare text. Each entry begins with an anchor , and the source title is a Markdown hyperlink to its URL

findings.why_it_matters: Combining two distinct sources into one numbered entry makes it impossible to cite them independently and obscures which claim each source individually supports. The methodology's format implies one source per numbered entry (the anchor-and-hyperlink model only accommodates a single URL per entry). The Connextra case is unavoidable since no stable URL exists for a 2001 conference-room practice; the Jeffries-plus-Cohn and Appcues-plus-AnnounceKit combinations are avoidable and create a citation precision gap. Books without URLs (Kent Beck ref-1 in user-stories) are a reasonable exception the methodology does not explicitly address.
findings.recommendation: Split combined entries: AC ref-3 should become two separate numbered entries (Jeffries, Cohn). RN ref-6 should become two entries (Appcues with its URL; AnnounceKit with its own URL). Add a paragraph to methodology section 6 explicitly handling print books (title, publisher, year, ISBN; no URL required) and pre-web practices (attribution, year, secondary source crediting the origin).

strengths: All four research logs exist and are explicitly honest about retrieval failures - 403 blocks for SVPG, a paywall for Lenny's Newsletter, and an empty render for the Scrum.org AC-vs-DoD page are each called out by name with the actual retrieval outcome. The logs distinguish 'Fetched, verified' from 'Search excerpt' from 'Search-corroborated' in a way that meets the methodology's own audit-grade standard, even when the companion's facing text does not surface that distinction.

The primary-tier sources that anchor the most load-bearing factual claims were successfully fetched and support their companion assertions: the Scrum Guide's three artifacts (Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment) confirm the 'no PRD in Scrum' claim; Keep a Changelog's six change types and anti-git-log-dump principle confirm the release-notes anatomy; SemVer 2.0.0 confirms the MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH semantics; Conventional Commits 1.0.0 confirms the feat/fix/BREAKING-CHANGE-to-version mapping; the Working Backwards website confirms Kindle and AWS and the pre-build PR/FAQ sequencing.

The inline citation coverage rate is 35 of 37 reference entries cited in body text (94.6 percent), and the citation mechanics - numbered anchors, hyperlinked titles, reliability tier tags - are consistently applied across all four bundles with only the minor format deviations noted in A-06.

The cross-family Saved Views example chain is internally consistent on the dimensions that matter most: personas (Recurring Analyst, Team Lead) are identical in PRD, user stories, and release notes; the open question about team defaults appears in the PRD open-questions table and is echoed in user-stories open questions and acceptance-criteria out-of-scope; the non-functional threshold (1 second at p95, WCAG 2.2 AA) is the same in PRD and acceptance-criteria example.

Methodology B4 compliance is clean: every illustrative figure in all four examples carries an explicit '(illustrative)' label - '12-person study' counts in the PRD, '3 story points (illustrative)' in user stories, '1 second at p95 (illustrative target)' in acceptance criteria, '30 percent faster at p95 (illustrative)' in release notes. No fabricated metrics are presented as real data.

The companion debate sections present genuine contested terrain rather than false consensus: the prototype-vs-PRD argument names Cagan as the proponent and gives an explicit recommendation; the AC-vs-DoD section correctly traces the distinction to both the Scrum Guide (DoD as a commitment) and the Scrum.org blog (AC ownership); the changelog-vs-release-notes distinction accurately cites Keep a Changelog's own framing of the difference.
ideas: Add a link-rot and version-staleness CI gate using lychee (https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee) to the bundle check workflow; configure it to flag both dead links and version-pinned citation URLs (paths matching /en/X.Y.Z/ for versioned specs) so a release with stale normative references fails the gate before shipping.

Introduce a machine-readable 'fetch_status' column in each research-log table (values: OK, 403, PAYWALL, EMPTY, CORROBORATED) and add a gate rule that any entry where fetch_status is not OK requires a qualifier in the companion reference entry (e.g., 'via search excerpt, direct fetch blocked'). This closes the gap between honest research logs and misleading companion citations systematically.

Pin normative standards citations (Keep a Changelog, SemVer, Conventional Commits, Scrum Guide) to a 'latest' redirect URL or the current version path rather than a specific older version, and add a DoD checklist item: 'verify that the cited version of every versioned standard is still the current release as of research date.'

Add a CI check for the reverse citation direction - every anchor in a companion's references section must have at least one inline [[n]] in the body - to catch padded entries that the current gate misses. This is a small extension of the existing citation-anchor check in check-bundles.py.

For the single-domain example limitation (documented as CT-1 in G6), add a brief scope note to each example file header explicitly stating the illustrative domain (B2B SaaS analytics) and noting that structural patterns apply across domains even if section content will differ; this makes the decided limitation visible to template users without waiting for a second domain example.

Consider adding a subscriber-access step to the research protocol for Tier-2 practitioner sources behind paywalls; the Lenny's Newsletter post is cited seven times in the PRD companion and is the most-cited Tier-2 source, yet its body was never read. One subscription would close the most frequent paywalled-source gap in the current bundle set.
audit.grade_rationale: The research underpinning this library is genuine: real fetches were performed, primary-tier sources were verified, the research logs are honest, and citation mechanics are correctly implemented for 33 of 37 entries. Two High failures - a citation that resolves but contradicts its attributed claim (Ranorex and the form-selection guidance) and three companion references asserting successful access for 403-blocked sources - introduce verifiable integrity gaps at the precise point where the 'researched, not remembered' core claim is made visible to readers. One factual error (Keep a Changelog version), two DoD-violating padded entries, and an unverifiable paywalled quotation add further medium-grade friction. The grade reflects real research work with specific, correctable failures rather than a systemic breakdown; the honest research logs and strong primary-source coverage prevent a lower grade.

verdicts.notes: All evidence holds. The companion excerpts at lines 24, 96, 122, and 124 are faithfully quoted. The live fetch of https://www.ranorex.com/blog/given-when-then-tests/ returned content confirming the article is a vendor-promotion piece that focuses exclusively on implementing GWT structure and promoting Ranorex tooling - it does not compare GWT scenarios against rule-based checklists, does not discuss form-selection, and does not mention that some teams default to checklists. Line 96 carries a solo [7] citation for the core guidance ("Default to the checklist; reach for scenarios when a flow is genuinely easier to read as Given/When/Then than as rules"), which the article provides zero support for. The research log (acceptance-criteria_research-log.md line 15) records Ranorex was retrieved via "Search excerpt" rather than a direct fetch, and incorrectly logs its claimed support as "When scenarios fit vs rule checklists." No governance document permits or ratifies this deviation; methodology section A4 explicitly prohibits paraphrasing a source into a stronger claim than it makes, and section A2 requires corroboration of vendor claims of fact against Tier 1 or 2 sources - which was not done. Severity High is appropriate: the citation resolves (making it appear credible), is invoked four to five times for the companion's central teaching point, and directly undermines the "researched, not remembered" integrity claim at its most instructive moment.
evidence_checked: _local/templates/acceptance-criteria/acceptance-criteria_research-log.md (full file, especially line 15 entry for source 7)
_local/templates/methodology.md sections A2, A4, A6, and 6 (Citation Standard)
https://www.ranorex.com/blog/given-when-then-tests/ (live fetch - article resolves; content does not compare checklist vs GWT forms)

verdicts.notes: All three evidence excerpts were verified at their exact line numbers. Companion lines 171, 173, and 175 each carry "(accessed 2026-06-30)" with no retrieval qualifier, while research-log line 12 and lines 27-29 explicitly document that the SVPG pages "returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch" and substance was "captured from search excerpts." A live re-fetch today of all three URLs - https://www.svpg.com/revisiting-the-product-spec/, https://www.svpg.com/discovery-vs-documentation/, and the PDF at the wp-content path - all returned HTTP 403 Forbidden, confirming the 403 condition is persistent rather than transient. Methodology section 6 specifies the format "URL (accessed YYYY-MM-DD)" with no documented exception or qualifier convention for blocked or paywalled sources; the word "403," "paywall," "blocked," and "search excerpt" do not appear anywhere in methodology.md. The session log (G10 line 97) lists the SVPG 403 issue as an unchecked open item, not a resolved decided deviation. No governance document authorizes using the standard "(accessed DATE)" format for sources not successfully retrieved. The finding's framing is accurate: the companion and the research log tell different stories about retrieval, the methodology provides no exception, and the three affected entries are the primary practitioner anchors for the library's core PRD-versus-prototype debate.

findings.title: Family contract file absent - plan AC-12 (family conformance) is violated

evidence.excerpt: AC-12 | A family contract exists and CI enforces family conformance | §11

`_families/delivery-docs.contract.md` - author the family contract per design spec §11: shared section vocabulary, required catalog-meta fields and allowed values for the family, the size-nesting expectation, and the shareable-boundary rule
## 11. Template Family Contract
family: delivery-docs (declared in prd, user-stories, acceptance-criteria, release-notes)

findings.why_it_matters: All four meta.yaml files declare `family: delivery-docs`, but `_families/delivery-docs.contract.md` does not exist and neither does the `_families/` directory. The plan's acceptance criterion AC-12 (G4 line 69) requires both the file and CI enforcement. Design spec G2 section 11 specifies the contract must define shared section vocabulary, required catalog-meta fields with allowed values, the nesting expectation, and the shareable-boundary rule. Without the contract, the family is a label with no machine-readable or human-reviewable definition. Conformance across the four bundles is enforced only by shared prose conventions in the methodology - a tacit contract that weakens as the family grows and cannot be gated in CI. This is the primary gap between the repo's stated governance model and its actual state.
findings.recommendation: Author `_families/delivery-docs.contract.md` per design spec G2 section 11. At minimum it should declare: (1) the phases and purpose that qualify a bundle for membership, (2) the required and optional meta.yaml fields with their allowed values (e.g. `phase: deliver`, `family: delivery-docs`, allowed `methodology` values), (3) the nesting rule (lean H2 a strict ordered subset of full H2), (4) the mandatory 11-section companion skeleton, (5) the Approach A guidance comment standard. Model the file on the pm-skills meeting-skills contract at `E:/Projects/product-on-purpose/pm-skills/site/src/content/docs/reference/skill-families/meeting-skills-contract.md` per the plan's own instruction (G4 line 295).

findings.title: No JSON Schema for meta.yaml - undeclared fields silently accepted and {{maintainer}} placeholder ships as a literal value

evidence.excerpt: def check_meta_sizes - uses a regex to parse sizes_available, not schema validation; no other field validation exists in any gate

`meta.yaml` carries catalog meta per the design spec: `id`, `title`, `summary`, `doc_type`, `phase` (lowercase), `family`, `sizes_available`, `methodology`, `pairs_with`, `status`, `template_version`, `tags`, `related_templates`, `last_reviewed`, `license` - no mention of `aliases`, `catalog_ref`, or `maintainer`
maintainer: "{{maintainer}}" - unfilled placeholder; same in all four meta.yaml files
`maintainer` present in design spec 9.1 table but absent from methodology B5 field list - governance inconsistency

findings.why_it_matters: No JSON Schema or equivalent exists for bundle metadata (confirmed by recursive file search across the entire repo). The gate's check F is a regex-based sizes_available comparison, not schema validation. Three fields present in all four meta.yaml files are undeclared in the methodology B5 field list (G1 line 139): `aliases`, `catalog_ref`, and `maintainer`. `maintainer` appears in design spec G2 section 9.1 but is absent from methodology G1 B5, so the two governance docs conflict. `aliases` and `catalog_ref` are in neither governance document. Additionally, `maintainer: "{{maintainer}}"` is an unfilled template placeholder in all four shipped metas. Any agent or tooling reading this field receives a literal string `{{maintainer}}` rather than an identity. Without a schema, undeclared fields accumulate silently and the placeholder is never caught.
findings.recommendation:
Create `_local/tools/meta.schema.json` as a JSON Schema (https://json-schema.org, draft 2020-12) that formalizes the meta.yaml field contract. Minimum fields: all 15 from the methodology B5 list, plus a deliberate decision on `aliases`, `catalog_ref`, and `maintainer` (either add them to the schema with types and constraints, or deprecate them with a migration note). Add schema validation to check-bundles.py as a new check G. Fill `{{maintainer}}` with an actual value (e.g., `product-on-purpose`) or remove it until a maintainer model is established. Stub schema:
```json
{"$schema":"https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema","title":"BundleMeta","type":"object","required":["id","title","summary","doc_type","phase","family","sizes_available","methodology","status","template_version","tags","last_reviewed","license"],"properties":{"id":{"type":"string"},"family":{"type":"string","enum":["delivery-docs"]},"phase":{"type":"string"},"sizes_available":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string","enum":["lean","full"]}},"status":{"type":"string","enum":["beta","stable","deprecated"]},"maintainer":{"type":"string","not":{"pattern":"\\{\\{"}}},"additionalProperties":false}}
```

strengths: All four bundles pass H2 ordered-subset nesting cleanly: prd (lean 7 in full 17), user-stories (lean 3 in full 7), acceptance-criteria (lean 3 in full 6), release-notes (lean 4 in full 11). No H3 headings exist in any variant, so no H3 nesting issues can arise. Shared H2 sections show only minor wording variation between lean and full variants; no case of same heading with diverged intent was found.

All 68 HTML comment blocks across the 8 template variant files (lean and full for all 4 bundles) carry all required fields: WHAT, WHY, ASK, GOOD, WEAK, and TRAP. The 'How to fill this in' preamble is present in all 8 variants inside an HTML comment. This is the highest-effort and most reader-visible quality signal in the bundle contract, and it is complete.

Every guide file exceeds the methodology minimum of 2 named anti-patterns by a factor of 3: each guide has exactly 6 named and numbered anti-patterns in bold, covering the most important failure modes. The guide structure is consistent across all 4 bundles (When to use, When NOT to use, Pick a variant, Quality rubric, Named anti-patterns).

All four companion files contain all 11 required skeleton sections per methodology G1 section 5: Orientation through References. No section is dropped or merged. Section numbering is consistent across all four companions.
History files are complete for all four bundles: each has a v0.1.0 entry that includes the research date (2026-06-30) and a substantive changelog note describing what was built and how.

Link integrity across all 42 tracked .md files is clean: 69 non-external links and 24 anchor links were checked; 0 genuine broken file links and 0 genuine broken anchor links were found. The methodology's intra-file anchors (#5-companion-document-structure, #6-citation-standard) resolve correctly. The implementation plan's #open-questions--decisions anchor is correct per the GitHub Markdown anchor algorithm.

The gate (check-bundles.py) is honest about its scope: its docstring explicitly lists its 6 checks, its code is readable and self-documenting, and it runs against all 4 bundles from a single command. Its documented limitations (H3 not checked, reverse citation direction not checked) are acknowledged rather than hidden.

Prose pointer accuracy is strong across 3 of 4 bundles: all pointers in user-stories, acceptance-criteria, and release-notes variants resolve correctly to named content in the corresponding companions. The single inaccuracy (NFRs abbreviation in prd full) is a minor label mismatch, not a pointer to a missing section.
ideas: Add a gate check (G) that validates meta.yaml against a JSON Schema. This single change would catch undeclared fields, placeholder values, and type errors mechanically on every future bundle. Pairing it with `yamllint` would also catch syntax errors.

Add a reverse-citation check to gate check E: confirm `anchors - cited_inline == empty set`. The one-line logic is already adjacent to the existing check. This would have caught the two uncited prd reference entries.

Add a `related_templates` resolver to the gate: build a list of known bundle IDs from the `_local/templates/` directory and flag any `related_templates` entry not in that list (or null). This mirrors how `pairs_with` is intended to be validated against pm-skills skill IDs.

Consider adding a ROW HINT presence check to the gate for sections that contain markdown tables: if the section body has a `|` line but the comment does not contain the string `ROW HINT`, fail the check.

Once the family contract file exists, it would be a natural home for the allowed-values vocabulary currently scattered across the methodology and design spec (e.g., allowed `phase` values, allowed `status` values, allowed `sizes_available` values). A companion JSON Schema could then be auto-derived from the contract.

The `aliases` field in all 4 metas is a high-value undeclared field that would power discovery and the alias-index.json planned in P7. Formalizing it in the schema now would prevent future format inconsistency across the 28 Tier-1 bundles.
audit.grade_rationale: The per-file structural foundations are genuinely strong: nesting passes cleanly across all 4 bundles, all 68 guidance comment blocks are complete, all companions have all 11 skeleton sections, all guides have 6 named anti-patterns (3x the minimum), and link integrity is clean. These are the hardest quality signals to fake and they pass without exception. The grade is held to B rather than A by two High findings: the family contract file required by plan AC-12 (G4 line 69) was never authored, leaving the `delivery-docs` family declaration in 4 metas without a governing document; and no JSON Schema or equivalent exists for meta.yaml, allowing undeclared fields and an unfilled `{{maintainer}}` placeholder to ship in all 4 bundles. Both gaps are acknowledged in the plan as 'Not started' rather than claimed complete, which confirms they are real governance debt rather than silent drift, but they still represent the gap between the repo's stated governance model and its actual state.

findings.title: No machine path from blank template to validated filled document; LP-1 (use-template flow) is unbuilt

evidence.excerpt: HOW TO FILL THIS IN ... 4. Before you ship: self-grade against prd_guide.md, then DELETE every HTML comment. They are guidance, not content.

LP-1 (use-template flow). The single most important unshipped thing: nothing has ever been filled from a template. Build a use-template flow... This resolves open decision D2 and is where all the value is actually realized.
The template layer that humans and AI agents both reach for first: governed, self-describing, sized to context, and traceable.
Agent-native | Consistent skeletons, machine-readable manifests, and a stable placeholder convention make the library directly consumable by AI agents, not just humans.

findings.why_it_matters: The repo's primary agent-native claim is that agents can use the library directly and reach for it first. In practice there is no MCP server, no CLI, no skill, and no interview flow; the only path is a human reading HTML comments and manually deleting them. G6 LP-1 explicitly calls this 'the single most important unshipped thing.' The enriched comments ARE a latent interview script (WHAT/ASK fields per section) and check-bundles.py check D is a latent validator (no remaining placeholders), but no code connects them. This is a documented-but-unbuilt gap, not silent drift; however the marketing copy in README line 3 and the design spec agent-native value pillar present the capability as current, not aspirational.
findings.recommendation: Build LP-1 as a thin agent skill or CLI: read meta.yaml to confirm bundle; read the chosen variant; extract the text of each section's ASK lines from HTML comments (regex `ASK\s+(.*?)(?=GOOD|WEAK|TRAP|-->)`) to drive a section-by-section interview; fill placeholders; add fill_date and filled_by to frontmatter; strip comments; run check D. Most ingredients exist. The MCP path (AG-2) can follow once LP-1 proves the flow.

findings.why_it_matters: The README presents pairs_with as the active bridge between the template library and pm-skills. The four declared values DO resolve to real skill directories and skill-manifest.json entries (deliver-prd, deliver-user-stories, deliver-acceptance-criteria, deliver-release-notes are all present and confirmed), so the intent is correctly targeted but the seam is entirely decorative. Additionally, two related_templates entries dangle: prd references 'solution-brief' (no bundle exists; develop-solution-brief skill exists in pm-skills) and release-notes references 'launch-checklist' (no bundle; deliver-launch-checklist skill exists). Remove or qualify the two dangling related_templates entries (solution-brief, launch-checklist) until bundles exist, or annotate them as 'planned'. Update README to qualify pairs_with as a declared intent pending activation rather than a live seam.

findings.why_it_matters: An agent selecting a template must: (1) glob _local/templates/ to discover bundle directories, (2) construct a filename like {bundle}/{bundle}_meta.yaml for each, (3) issue 4 separate reads, (4) parse YAML with no schema to validate against. There is no JSON Schema for meta.yaml, meaning field names, types, required vs optional, and allowed values are inferred from example files. The methodology B5 list of meta fields (line 138) omits two fields that appear in every shipped meta.yaml (maintainer, aliases), showing the spec and the artifact have already drifted. Against the Anthropic tool design principle 'return only high signal information' and 'tool consolidation' (verified at anthropic.com/engineering/writing-tools-for-agents), four separate reads with no schema is structurally inefficient.
findings.recommendation: Write manifest.json at the repo root listing all bundle metas as an array (one object per bundle, all current meta fields). Generate it from the 4 YAML files via a script so it stays in sync. Add a JSON Schema file (meta-schema.json) documenting required fields, types, and allowed enum values. Add a gate check that validates each meta.yaml against the schema and verifies the manifest is current.

findings.title: Size selection is not deterministic from meta fields alone; no sizing_guidance, default_size, or when_lean field exists

evidence.excerpt: sizes_available: [lean, full]
sizes_available: [lean, full]
Lean is the minimum genuinely useful version. Full is comprehensive and a strict superset.

findings.why_it_matters: C1 simulation: (1) 'announce a shipped feature to customers' resolves deterministically to release-notes via aliases ('release announcement') and summary, but lean vs full cannot be determined from meta fields alone. (2) 'rough feature idea, small effort' resolves deterministically to prd via summary ('defines what to build... why'), but 'small effort' does not map to any meta field; 'one-pager' in aliases hints at lean but is not a decision rule. (3) 'define pass/fail conditions for a story' resolves deterministically to acceptance-criteria via summary. Bundle selection succeeds in all 3 cases from meta fields; size selection fails in all 3. An agent must either default to lean (not declared), read the companion (3K-5K tokens), read the guide (500-700 tokens), or ask a clarifying question. None of these paths are specified in the meta.
findings.recommendation: Add a sizing_guidance string to each meta.yaml (for example: 'lean for single feature or quick spike; full for multi-team initiative, regulated context, or high-ambiguity scope') and a default_size field (lean for all 4 current bundles). This allows an agent to select a size deterministically from the meta alone and only escalate to companion/guide when the user context is genuinely ambiguous.

A YAML parser returns this as a string, not as a missing-value signal; an agent acting on it would use '{{maintainer}}' as the maintainer name. findings.title: No token or file-size metadata in meta.yaml; agents cannot budget context consumption before fetching bundle files

evidence.excerpt: [no approx_tokens, no file_sizes, no token_budget field; all 20 lines confirmed]
[21387 bytes, approx 5346 tokens at 4 chars/token - largest file in the library]
[16798 bytes, approx 4199 tokens - a full template with guidance comments]

findings.why_it_matters: Measured file sizes across all 4 bundles: companion files range 12,698-21,387 bytes (approx 3,174-5,346 tokens); full templates range 7,926-16,798 bytes (approx 1,981-4,199 tokens); lean templates range 4,076-7,193 bytes (approx 1,019-1,798 tokens). An agent selecting a template has no machine-readable signal for how much context budget a given file role will consume. Fetching a companion + full template for PRD costs roughly 9,500 tokens before any user content is added. The Anthropic tool design principle (verified) of 'returning only high signal information' and 'context efficiency' both argue for giving an agent the budget signal upfront. Without it, a constrained agent may inadvertently exhaust its context window or, conversely, over-restrict its fetching unnecessarily.
findings.recommendation: Add an approx_tokens map to each meta.yaml with per-file-role estimates (companion, template_lean, template_full, guide, example), auto-generated by a script from actual byte counts at / 4. This gives an agent a single-read budget signal before it fetches any content, enabling it to choose guide (approx 600 tokens) over companion (approx 5,000 tokens) when context is tight.

findings.title: No fill provenance fields (filled_by, fill_date, fill_method) in template instance frontmatter; agent fills are unattributed

evidence.excerpt: source_template: prd / source_template_version: 0.1.0 [present; no filled_by, fill_date, or fill_method field]
source_template: prd / source_template_version: 0.1.0 [verified present; created: '2026-06-12'; no filled_by field]
Provenance and traceability | A filled document carries a stamp back to its source template and version. You can answer 'which live docs run on a deprecated template.'

findings.why_it_matters: The design spec's provenance value pillar is partially met: source_template and source_template_version are present and consistent across all 4 examples and verified in all variant files. But when a filled document needs to record who (or what agent) filled it and when, there is no field for it. The {{date}} placeholder in created/updated serves as an approximate fill-date proxy but is user-supplied rather than auto-stamped, and nothing distinguishes a human fill from an agent fill. As LP-1 is built, agent-fill provenance becomes auditable only if the frontmatter schema has a home for it.
findings.recommendation: Add filled_by: '{{filled_by}}' and fill_method: '{{fill_method}}' (values: 'manual' or 'agent') placeholder fields to both template variants. The strip script (per C-09) can auto-fill fill_date at strip time. This costs one line per template and enables future tooling to answer 'which docs in this repo were agent-filled on which template version'.

findings.title: Guidance-strip is a manual human step with no script; comments left in place add ~1,000-3,200 tokens of noise if an agent consumes a partially-filled document

evidence.excerpt: 4. Before you ship: self-grade against prd_guide.md, then DELETE every HTML comment. They are guidance, not content.
4. Before you ship: self-grade against prd_guide.md, then DELETE every HTML comment.
[no strip script exists; only check-bundles.py present in _local/tools/]

findings.why_it_matters: The prd lean template contains approximately 1,798 total tokens, of which guidance comments account for a significant fraction (the template-full is 4,199 tokens, of which roughly 2,400 are guidance comments vs. structural content). If an agent receives a partially-filled document with comments still in place, it must parse and ignore irrelevant guidance noise. The comment syntax `<!-- ... -->` is uniform and fully automatable with a standard regex. A 20-line Python script (similar in structure to check-bundles.py) would strip all HTML comments and write the clean file. The absence of such a script means every author - human or agent - must do this manually, and a gate check does not exist to catch shipped documents with residual comments.
findings.recommendation: Add strip-template.py to _local/tools/ that reads a filled file, removes all HTML comment blocks via regex, auto-fills fill_date in frontmatter, and writes the clean output. Wire it as a suggested final step in the fill preamble: 'Run python _local/tools/strip-template.py <file> to remove guidance comments and stamp the fill date.' Optionally add a gate check that warns when a filled document contains `<!--` blocks.

strengths: All 4 pairs_with values resolve correctly to real skill directories (deliver-prd, deliver-user-stories, deliver-acceptance-criteria, deliver-release-notes) and to entries in pm-skills/skill-manifest.json - the seam is correctly targeted even though not yet wired; an agent following the field lands on real, functional skills.

source_template and source_template_version provenance fields are present and consistent across all 4 worked examples and all variant files (verified in prd, user-stories, acceptance-criteria, release-notes examples); the template version stamping that backs the design spec's provenance value pillar is genuinely implemented.

Lean template token budgets are context-efficient: the lean variants range from approximately 1,019 to 1,798 tokens, small enough to travel alongside a drafting task, a system prompt, and user content in any production context window.

The alias field enables intent-based agent selection: 3 out of 3 simulated intents resolved to the correct bundle deterministically using only alias and summary fields. 'release announcement' maps intent 1; 'one-pager' and the summary phrase 'what to build, for whom, and why' map intent 2; 'conditions of satisfaction' and the summary phrase 'conditions a user story must satisfy to be accepted' map intent 3.

The {{snake_case}} placeholder convention is declared in README line 110, codified in methodology B1 line 113, and machine-enforced by gate check D (no placeholders left in example) - an agent has a clean, uniform substitution target across all bundle files.

HTML comment guidance blocks use a syntactically uniform structure (WHAT / WHY / ASK / GOOD / WEAK / TRAP labeling), making the comment interior parseable by regex. The ASK fields in particular constitute a latent per-section interview script that an LP-1 (use-template) implementation could consume with no authoring duplication.

catalog_ref integer values are verified correct against the master catalog: 29 = PRD, 30 = User Story, 38 = Acceptance Criteria, 115 = Release Notes - the cross-reference is accurate even if the machine path to it is not encoded in the field itself.
ideas: manifest.json at repo root: a single JSON file indexing all bundle metas (auto-generated from YAML files by a build script) would cut agent bundle enumeration from 4 separate reads to 1, and is already planned in P5.

sizing_guidance string and default_size field in meta.yaml: a single sentence (e.g. 'lean for single feature or spike; full for multi-team or regulated scope') plus a declared default makes size selection deterministic without requiring a companion read.

approx_tokens map in meta.yaml per file role (companion, template_lean, template_full, guide, example): auto-generated from byte counts at divide-by-4, gives agents a budget signal before fetching; aligns with the Anthropic tool design principle of context efficiency.

LP-1 (use-template) as a thin agent skill: parse WHAT and ASK lines from each HTML comment using a regex, drive a section-by-section interview, fill placeholders, call strip-template.py, write provenance. All ingredients exist; LP-1 is an orchestration layer, not a content build.

For a future MCP server (AG-2): define a bundle-summary resource type returning guide + meta only (approx 800-1,000 tokens total) as the initial response, with companion available as a separate resource on request. This respects the Anthropic principle of 'pagination and range selection' and avoids loading 5K-token companions for every selection decision.

fill_date + filled_by + fill_method placeholder fields in template frontmatter cost one additional line per variant and enable future tooling to audit 'which live docs were agent-filled on which template version' - a natural extension of the existing provenance design.

A 20-30 line strip-template.py script in _local/tools/ completes the existing toolset: read the filled file, strip `<!-- ... -->` blocks via regex, auto-stamp fill_date, write clean output; wire it as the final step after check-bundles.py.

AG-1 (machine-readable section schema) from G6: adding a per-template section schema (required vs optional sections, field types, table columns) as structured YAML in meta.yaml would let an agent self-check completeness after filling - the next natural layer above the placeholder convention.
audit.grade_rationale: The architectural groundwork for agent-native readiness is genuine and well-considered: consistent {{snake_case}} placeholders, parseable HTML comment guidance, correct pairs_with targets, alias fields that enable deterministic bundle selection, and lean templates sized for context windows. These are real assets, not marketing claims. However, the agent-native value pillar is materially unimplemented against the repo's own stated bar ('humans and AI agents both reach for it first'): there is no machine path to fill a template (LP-1 unbuilt), pairs_with is a decorative seam with zero consumers on either side, no catalog manifest exists for enumeration, size selection is non-deterministic from meta fields, and all 4 machine manifests ship with a literal '{{maintainer}}' placeholder. G6 itself candidly acknowledges the repo is 'not yet reachable by the agents the vision names as first-class users'; the grade reflects that honest gap between a sound architecture and a functional agent-consumable library.

findings.title: Nesting check examines H2 headings only - H3 reordering and semantic heading drift are invisible to the gate

evidence.excerpt: HEADING_RE = re.compile(r'^##\s+(.*?)\s*$', re.MULTILINE) - matches ## only, not ###
def headings(path): return HEADING_RE.findall(read(path)) - used by check_nesting
Lean is the minimum genuinely useful version. Full is comprehensive and a strict superset: every lean section ID appears in full, unchanged in name and order

findings.why_it_matters: The DoD clause says 'section IDs nest (smaller is a strict subset of larger)' and methodology B1 says 'unchanged in name and order.' The gate enforces this at H2 level only. Currently all four bundles use only H2 headings in templates (verified), so no false negatives exist today. But future bundles could add H3 sub-sections; a renamed or reordered H3 between lean and full would silently pass. The check also does not detect semantic drift - a lean heading 'Problem Statement' renamed to 'Problem' in full would pass the ordered-subset test because neither is a subset of the other (the check catches this) but 'Problem Statement' and 'Problem Statement (see companion)' would both pass as matching strings. This is a boundary condition worth noting, not a current defect.
findings.recommendation: Extend HEADING_RE to capture all headings (##+ using r'^#{2,}\s+(.*?)\s*$') and make is_ordered_subset compare the tuples (level, text) rather than text alone. This catches H3+ reordering without changing the logic.

findings.why_it_matters: All four bundles declare template_version: 0.1.0 in their meta.yaml files, and per-bundle HISTORY.md files exist. But no git tag v0.1.0 pins that version to a commit, and no repo-level CHANGELOG.md exists. A consumer (human or agent) who wants to pin their use of the library to the version the bundles advertise, or who wants to see the diff between 0.1.0 and a future 0.2.0, has nothing to reference. This undermines the provenance and traceability claims that are the stated moat: the meta.yaml provenance fields (source_template_version) will flow into filled documents, but there is no external anchor that confirms what 0.1.0 actually was. The design spec section 14 and plan P5 both required this; the gap is silent drift against those commitments.
findings.recommendation: After adding the LICENSE file (E-01), create a CHANGELOG.md with a single [0.1.0] section listing the four bundles. Tag the resulting commit v0.1.0. This takes under an hour and completes the version traceability chain the design spec committed to.

strengths: The adoption sequencing documented in G6 (LP-2 grade-my-existing-doc as the first top-of-funnel wedge, before infrastructure investment) is a genuinely well-reasoned strategy. G6 section 6 applies YAGNI correctly: 'Do not build them for a library nobody uses yet. The correct order is LP-2/LP-1 first (get real usage and real feedback), then invest in the heavy machinery the usage justifies.' This is a stronger sequencing argument than the typical infra-first instinct.

The sibling pm-skills repo provides a complete, proven reference for every missing distribution surface (plugin.json, marketplace.json, AGENTS.md, CI workflows, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING). The gap is execution against a known pattern, not design uncertainty - which means the remediation path is mechanical and low-risk.

The _local/ location deviation is documented and reasoned rather than silent drift. The .gitignore comment (lines 1-5), README line 69, and methodology line 8 all explain the choice explicitly, naming it a deliberate deferred decision. This means graduation is a traceable task with known stakeholder context, not a mystery.

The pull-based roadmap model (catalog Recommendation 1, P7 design) is the right architecture for a solo maintainer at an early stage: building only what real teams explicitly request prevents the 224-file Tier 1 backlog from becoming an open obligation and keeps quality high on a smaller set.
ideas: LP-2 sequencing verdict (E4 adoption wedge): LP-2 is defensible as the first adoption move. It demands the lowest user commitment (paste an existing doc vs. fill a blank page), reuses the rubric already written in each bundle's guide, and can be delivered as a single pm-skill before any distribution infrastructure exists. The correct sequence is: resolve D2/D3 this week, add LICENSE file, build LP-2 as a pm-skill to get real usage signal, then LP-1, then AG-2/MCP only once usage justifies the infrastructure cost. Plugin-first or MCP-first would be high-effort premature optimization at the current zero-usage, zero-real-consumer state.

The meta.yaml maintainer field carries a persistent unfilled placeholder (prd_meta.yaml line 18: maintainer: '{{maintainer}}'). Catalog metadata describes the template as a library asset (design spec section 9.1), so the maintainer is the library owner, not the document consumer. The placeholder has no consumer to fill it - any agent querying the library's authorship gets a substitution token. Replace with 'product-on-purpose' across all four meta.yaml files.

VL-1 (business model) does not need full resolution before first external distribution. The minimal viable position is 'free and open, same as pm-skills, funded via product-on-purpose consulting' (VL-2 in G6). Deferring VL-1 past first external sharing risks the library being distributed without any business model context at all, which is harder to walk back than choosing a position and revising it.

The _working/ folder (context sheet section 2, G9) documents its own deletion: 'Delete _working/ once you have decided; the A/B/C guidance-style decision was made (Approach A adopted). The folder still exists in the tracked tree. Deleting it removes a confusing historical artifact from the front door of the library.
audit.grade_rationale: Against the repo's own governance standard, the distribution dimension is in the deepest deficit. The design spec (section 12) committed to six distribution channels; the plan (G4) committed to plugin manifests, CI, CHANGELOG, version tags, AGENTS.md, and CONTRIBUTING.md; zero of these are built. The LICENSE file is absent despite Apache-2.0 being declared in every meta.yaml and the README, creating a Critical legal gap for a copy-and-fill template library. The sustainability math at Tier 1 scale (224 files, ~259 citations, solo maintainer, no funding plan) has no documented path to closure. The grade is D rather than F because the gaps are broadly acknowledged in the governance corpus (P5/P6 'Not started' in G4's own progress table, G6 section 3.G explicitly naming the business-model silence, the YAGNI sequencing argument in G6 section 6 being genuinely sound), the remediation plan is detailed and correct, and the content quality that distribution would carry earns an A on its own terms - this is a strong library that has not yet been distributed, not a weak library that cannot be improved."

findings.why_it_matters: Diataxis (https://diataxis.fr - retrieval returned HTTP 429; framework described from documented knowledge) requires a tutorial quadrant: a learning-oriented experience that guides a newcomer through their first success in a safe, controlled way. No tutorial exists here. A consumer arriving at the README can (a) find a bundle via the table at lines 29-34 and (b) understand bundle anatomy at lines 44-58, but (c) filling a first template correctly requires opening the raw file in an editor to see the preamble comment - a step the README never mentions, demonstrates, or links. The only fill-in instructions are inside prd_template-lean.md:15-28, visible only when the file is open in an editor. The README at line 21 points to the methodology - which is the contributor authoring process, not a consumer how-to. A documentation product that cannot onboard its own first user from its front door undermines the 'best-in-class' claim at the most basic interaction.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Quick start: use a template' section to README.md (or a linked docs/quickstart-use-a-template.md) with literal steps: (1) clone or download the repo; (2) open _local/templates/prd/prd_template-lean.md in your editor; (3) read the preamble comment and each section's guidance; (4) replace placeholders; (5) self-grade against prd_guide.md; (6) delete all HTML comments before sharing. ideas: Target doc tree for Documentation Rebuild Plan (from F-06, grade below A). Consumer path: (1) README.md under 200 lines, consumer-first with a 'Quick start' block and no contributor sections; (2) docs/quickstart-use-a-template.md - tutorial with literal steps: clone, open lean template in editor, read preamble, replace placeholders, self-grade against guide, delete comments; (3) _local/templates/prd/README.md - per-bundle landing: what a PRD is, lean vs full signal, links to guide/companion/example; (4) same per-bundle README for user-stories, acceptance-criteria, release-notes. Contributor path: (5) CONTRIBUTING.md - authoring overview, pointers to methodology and ADRs; (6) docs/contributing/authoring-contract.md - single reference home consolidating 8-file bundle anatomy, citation standard (G1 section 6), comment standard (B1 labels), nesting rule, gate checks A-F with limitations, meta field schema, family contract requirements, DoD 11 clauses; (7) docs/decisions/ directory; (8) docs/decisions/20260629-repo-and-package-name.md (status: accepted); (9) docs/decisions/20260629-variant-model.md (status: accepted); (10) docs/decisions/20260629-phase-vocabulary.md (status: accepted); (11) docs/decisions/20260629-first-family-and-bundle.md (status: accepted); (12) docs/decisions/20260702-guidance-style.md (status: accepted, records Approach A selection); (13) docs/decisions/20260702-research-log-as-8th-bundle-file.md (status: accepted); (14) docs/decisions/20260703-gate-implementation.md (status: interim, Python/local vs Node/CI); (15) docs/decisions/TEMPLATE.md (MADR stub, see below); (16) docs/reference/gate-checks.md (checks A-F, scope, known gap: E is inline-to-anchor only); (17) docs/reference/metadata-schema.md (catalog meta and instance meta fields with allowed values); (18) docs/reference/family-contract.md (delivery-docs family contract, fulfilling plan AC-12 and design-spec section 11).

Ready-to-commit MADR-style template stub for docs/decisions/TEMPLATE.md (under 30 lines, confirmed minimal MADR format from https://adr.github.io/madr/ HTTP 200 retrieval, minimal example approximately 13 lines): '# [Title: short, imperative phrase describing what was decided] / / Status: [proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded-by YYYYMMDD-title.md] / Date: YYYY-MM-DD / Deciders: [names] / / ## Context and Problem Statement / / [Describe the situation and the question that required a decision. One to three sentences.] / / ## Considered Options / / * Option A: ... / * Option B: ... / / ## Decision Outcome / / Chosen option: Option A, because [one-line justification]. / / ### Consequences / / * Good: [positive outcome] / * Accepted risk: [negative outcome or trade-off accepted]'. That is 22 lines including blank separator lines, under the 30-line limit.

Progressive disclosure review: the current README (117 lines) serves two interleaved audiences. A consumer needs only the first 40-50 lines (positioning, bundle table, anatomy, pm-skills relationship). A contributor needs the rest plus CONTRIBUTING.md. Restructuring to consumer-first progressive disclosure - positioning (10 lines), quick start (8 lines), what is here (10 lines), anatomy (12 lines), pm-skills relationship (4 lines), then a clear 'Going deeper' divider pointing to docs/ - would let a returning user find their template in the first scroll without reading governance content. The methodology.md line 53 already cites Diataxis ('the companion and the guide are deliberately separate (Diataxis split: explanation/reference vs how-to)') - applying that same self-awareness to the repo's own meta-docs would be internally consistent and credible.

audit.grade_rationale: The template preambles and companions are genuinely Diataxis-aware content - WHAT/WHY/ASK/GOOD/WEAK/TRAP maps to point-of-use how-to, companions map to explanation, and the methodology is a strong contributor reference. But the meta-documentation architecture fails three of the four Diataxis quadrants for its consumer audience: the tutorial quadrant is entirely absent (no quickstart path exists and the beginner's journey breaks at the critical 'fill first template' step), the reference quadrant is scattered across five documents in three deep paths with no docs/ tree, and the how-to quadrant is missing at the repo level even though it exists inside the raw template files. Held against the repo's own higher bar - a documentation product whose meta-documentation must model the standards it teaches - the structural failures in consumer onboarding, contributor reference architecture, and the absence of the four ADR files the plan's own blocking gate required push the grade to C despite the genuine strength of the content that does exist.

findings.why_it_matters: The single named moat is governed quality ('the methodology and design spec both name this explicitly' - G6:29). Stating 'enforceable, not aspirational' and 'CI-enforced' on the front door while enforcement is a hand-run local script with no CI, covering part of the DoD, is a credibility risk on a repo whose entire positioning is credibility. A reader who clones and finds no CI, no LICENSE, and a partial gate experiences the exact gap between claim and artifact that the repo exists to avoid.
findings.recommendation: Either close the gap or soften the claim, not neither. G6's own risk section contradicts its 80/20: 'Do not build them [evals, MCP, schema] for a library nobody uses yet. The correct order is LP-2/LP-1 first ... then invest in the heavy machinery' - and neither list includes the LICENSE/tags/install floor that gates any usage at all

findings.why_it_matters: Central-claim verdict (seeds the exec summary): 'governed, best-in-class, agent-native reference implementation' is PARTIALLY TRUE. Earned on the content axis: the four bundles are genuinely researched, dual-reader, sized-with-nesting, self-describing, and provenance-stamped - rare craft that the two named competitors (dend/awesome-product-management and lorabv/awesome-agile, both verified live as curated link-lists, not template collections) do not attempt. On credit: 'governed' (enforcement is a local prototype, not CI), 'agent-native' (no agent can consume it - no MCP, no section schema, no install path; the meta.yaml is machine-readable but has no machine consumer), and 'reference implementation' (it is 4 of 205 types, unlicensed, untagged, unreleased, living under _local/ - a high-quality prototype, not yet something others can point to and install). A wedge with no install path and no license converts nobody, no matter how good the evals.
findings.recommendation: Insert a distribution/credibility floor ahead of the heavy machinery. Cheapest high-leverage sequence: (1) add the LICENSE file (hours; closes a legal gap the repo already claims to have filled); (2) execute HY-2 (graduate _local/templates to a tracked path) and D2 (the '30-minute' skills-CLI install test) so there is a real install story; (3) cut a v0.1.0 tag + minimal CHANGELOG so the four finished bundles are a citable release. Only then LP-2 (grade-my-doc, which works on 4 bundles and generates the usage signal EV-1 needs), then EV-1, then AG-2 (MCP) last per G6's own YAGNI reasoning. This inverts the 80/20 headline in favor of G6's own risk-section logic.

findings.title: Strategic sustainability risks are named in the docs but left unowned: solo-maintainer bus factor, 37 citations with zero freshness automation, and a work-tree-only history for most of its life

evidence.excerpt: VL-3: 'Best-in-class content rots without a keeper. Decide the maintenance model now ... because FR-1 will generate a steady trickle of this-needs-re-checking that needs an owner' - decision still open, no cadence set

FR-1: 'Recency is a stated principle but has no automation' - 37 live citation URLs across four companions, no link-check, no staleness cron; methodology principle 4 ('Recency is a feature') is policy without enforcement

findings.why_it_matters: The repo's differentiation is researched, current, trustworthy content maintained to a high bar. That bar is a recurring cost, and every mechanism to sustain it (freshness automation, a maintenance cadence, a second maintainer) is either an open decision or unbuilt. Citation rot is silent: a companion that cited a live standard in June looks identical when the URL 404s in December. For a library whose whole claim is trust, unmanaged decay is the most likely way the claim quietly becomes false.
findings.recommendation: Convert two named risks into owned commitments now, cheaply: (a) decide VL-3 (even 'solo maintainer, quarterly re-verification pass' written down beats silence) and put a recurring calendar entry against it; (b) add a link-check over the 37 citation URLs to the same CI that runs the gate (FR-1's minimum viable form) so dead links fail loudly. Bus-factor is harder, but a CONTRIBUTING.md plus the agent-authoring-kit (AG-3, described as 'low effort, most of it exists as tacit process') lowers the cost of a second hand.

But the plan explicitly required durable, individually-addressable decision records as a blocking gate, and the traceability pointer at G4:381 now asserts ADRs exist that do not. Decisions scattered as inline amendments across two documents are harder to find, cite, and supersede than four MADR files, and the repo elsewhere (the sibling pm-skills, the user's own jp-init-project convention) treats MADR as standard. This is precisely the 'a real decision recorded only in a non-durable place' case the deviation rule flags as a finding.

strengths: The strategy corpus is unusually rigorous and self-aware, which is itself a strategic asset. G3 (strategy brief, catalog-to-library) reasons explicitly through a reversibility lens (line 73: commit hard on expensive-to-reverse decisions, stay uncommitted on cheap ones) and names Approach B (coverage-first) as 'the seductive wrong answer' (line 113) before rejecting it. G6 (raising-the-ceiling brief) opens by stating its own central gap plainly: 'best-in-class is asserted, not proven' (line 38). A repo that audits its own weaknesses this honestly is far more trustworthy than one that does not.

The four expensive-to-reverse decisions were settled before any content was written, exactly as the reversibility discipline prescribes, and the built artifacts match them. Verified: prd_meta.yaml uses phase: deliver (lowercase, line 7), sizes_available: [lean, full] (line 9), family: delivery-docs (line 8), id-per-bundle naming - all consistent with the 2026-06-29 resolutions in G3 section 5. This is the single highest-leverage sequencing call and it was made correctly.

The differentiation claim against the named competitive set is true and verifiable, not marketing. Both competitor URLs the design spec cites (github.com/dend/awesome-product-management and github.com/lorabv/awesome-agile) resolve live and are confirmed curated link-lists pointing outward to scattered external resources, not governed template collections. The design spec's framing (section 2, line 37: 'Curated lists ... point outward to scattered resources. The genuinely high-quality fillable-templates-in-one-governed-repo does not exist') holds up under direct inspection.

The content-axis of the moat is genuinely built today, not promised: all four bundles carry provenance frontmatter, tiered/reliability-tagged citations, lean/full nesting, and machine-readable meta.yaml, and the local gate passes all six structural checks on all four. The dual-reader companion/guide split (companion for the why, guide for the how) is a real differentiator that the awesome-lists do not attempt.

G6's risk section (line 154+) already contains the correct sequencing instinct that its own 80/20 headline understates: 'Do not build them for a library nobody uses yet ... LP-2/LP-1 first ... then invest in the heavy machinery the usage justifies.' The right answer to the roadmap stress test is already latent in the corpus; it just needs to be promoted over the flashier evals/MCP headline.
ideas: Add a single 'STATE.md' or a status block at the top of the README that is the one source of truth for what is built vs planned (4 of 205 bundles, 1 family, local gate, no CI/tags/license-file yet). Right now that truth is scattered across a stale plan table, a README that reads as finished, and a G6 brief - a reader cannot get an accurate current-state snapshot from any single file.

Treat 'we deliberately did not build this' as a first-class, visible state (G6 VS-3 already argues this). The atlas renders all 205 types; marking the ~201 not-built as an explicit pull-gated decision - rather than an implicit backlog - turns the coverage gap from a weakness into evidence of discipline, which strengthens rather than undercuts the best-in-class claim.

Resolve the D2 skills-CLI install test (G3 calls it a 30-minute test, open 11 days) before writing any more roadmap prose, because its outcome determines whether a use-template companion skill is a required part of the distribution story or optional - and that single fact reorders LP-1, AG-2, and the P5 distribution work.

Consider making the pm-skills flywheel (G6 VS-2) a literal diagram in the README: skill decides how, template is the what, filled doc feeds the next skill. The pairs_with seam is currently prose-only and 'decorative' (no validator here, no consumer there per the context sheet); a diagram plus a validator that resolves pairs_with against the pinned skill-ID list would make the ecosystem-coherence claim concrete rather than asserted.
audit.grade_rationale: The strategic reasoning is genuinely A-grade: rigorous, self-aware, with the expensive-to-reverse decisions settled correctly before content and the competitive differentiation verified as real. But this dimension measures coherence between the stated strategy and the built reality, and that coherence has drifted: the operative plan reads Not-started-x7 while calling itself the source of truth, the 'governed / CI-enforced' front-door claim outruns a local-only prototype with no CI or LICENSE file, and the roadmap prioritizes proving and reaching quality (evals, MCP) while the library remains unlicensed, untagged, and unreachable by any consumer. The central claim - 'governed, best-in-class, agent-native reference implementation' - is partially true: the content craft is earned, but 'governed' (enforcement is a prototype), 'agent-native' (no agent can consume it), and 'reference implementation' (4 of 205, under _local/, no release) are still on credit.
